narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to pursue full-time studies, you've put real effort into your law school application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicted an 88.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was unfortunately rejected—schools rely on this to ensure they admit students likely to succeed long-term. This prediction stemmed primarily from your academic performance in school, where your Year 3 grades placed you in the lowest decile compared to your peers, making it the single biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure. Your Year 1 grades, also in the lowest decile, reinforced this concern as the second-most influential element, signaling to the model a consistent pattern that worried it about your bar exam readiness. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 was a bit below the typical 3.21 average, it actually worked slightly in your favor, nudging the prediction toward success rather than failure. Your LSAT score of 29, lower than the average of about 37, had a minor pull toward the failure prediction but wasn't a dominant driver. Factors like your middle-income bracket and full-time enrollment status had almost no impact, either positive or negative, in the model's eyes. Compared to typical students in the dataset—who often rank in the middle or higher deciles for grades, with stronger LSATs and GPAs—your profile stood out as riskier specifically due to those early low decile rankings. This doesn't define your potential, but it explains the model's cautious stance. To move forward, I recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with updated transcripts that show clear improvement. You're capable of turning this around—many students in similar situations have succeeded by focusing on these areas. Keep pushing; your determination as a white male from a middle-class background positions you well to overcome this hurdle with targeted effort. We're rooting for you.
